content.text: When building agentic AI systems, the wrong database choice creates friction that compounds with every feature added: storing nested conversation blobs in rigid SQL columns, running similarity searches with LIKE operators, or caching hot session state on disk. NoSQL databases solve these specific access patterns through a family of purpose-built engines — key-value stores, document databases, wide-column stores, and vector indexes — each optimized for a distinct query shape that relational systems handle poorly at scale.

NoSQL is not a single technology — it is an umbrella term for databases that deliberately trade the relational model's strict schema and ACID transaction guarantees for gains in flexibility, horizontal scalability, or specialized query performance. The four main families are:
(Pinecone, Weaviate, Qdrant, and pgvector as a SQL extension).

Each family has a distinct data model and indexing strategy. Key-value stores expose a hash map: O(1) get/set by primary key. Document stores index and query nested JSON objects without a predefined schema. Wide-column stores organize data as sparse, distributed rows with many optional columns — ideal for time-series and write-heavy workloads. Vector databases store high-dimensional float arrays and answer approximate nearest-neighbor (ANN) queries using specialized indexes such as HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World) or IVF-Flat.

The "NoSQL" label emerged around 2009 as a shorthand for the class of databases shown to work at web scale (Cassandra at Facebook, Bigtable at Google, Dynamo at Amazon). Today most production systems use them alongside, not instead of, relational databases — a pattern called polyglot persistence.

Agent systems generate data shapes that SQL handles awkwardly. A single conversation turn can be a nested JSON object containing a user message, an array of tool calls, each tool call's structured arguments, and the tool response. Normalizing this into a relational schema requires five or more joined tables and reconstruction logic on every read — overhead that adds latency and complexity without benefit.

The retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pattern — feeding the agent relevant documents at inference time — requires similarity search over hundreds of thousands of embedding vectors. SQL has no native similarity operator; a brute-force cosine distance scan in Postgres is O(n) and becomes unacceptably slow past roughly 100k vectors. A dedicated vector index (HNSW) reduces query latency from seconds to single-digit milliseconds at million-vector scale.

Session cache and rate limiting require sub-millisecond reads and atomic increments. SQL row locks and commit overhead make these workloads unnecessarily expensive. Redis, with its in-memory hash map and native INCR/EXPIRE commands, handles millions of such operations per second on modest hardware.
— document stores accept any JSON structure without migrations; useful for evolving agent message formats.
— DynamoDB and Cassandra partition data across nodes and scale write throughput by adding capacity; relational primary keys become bottlenecks first.
— Redis keeps the hot dataset in RAM; session load and rate-limit checks return in under 1 ms.
— HNSW-indexed vector databases find the top-k most similar embeddings in single-digit milliseconds at million-vector scale.
— DynamoDB on-demand, Firestore, Pinecone Serverless, and Upstash Redis eliminate capacity planning for spiky agent workloads.
— built-in expiry on keys (Redis EXPIRE, DynamoDB TTL attribute) automatically purge stale session state without cron jobs.
— Cassandra and Bigtable's clustering columns let an agent append events and later retrieve a time window without full-table scans.
— document and KV stores lack joins and GROUP BY; analytics queries require ETL to a data warehouse.
— many NoSQL systems default to eventual consistency; writing an idempotency key and immediately reading it back may return stale data, causing duplicate tool execution.
— running Redis, Cassandra, and a vector index alongside Postgres means multiple on-call runbooks, monitoring dashboards, and backup strategies.
— DynamoDB's partition and sort key design is baked in at table creation; changing the primary access pattern requires rebuilding the table.
— committing related state atomically across Redis and Postgres requires application-level saga patterns or two-phase commit, both complex to get right.
Cold start on serverless vector DBs
— Pinecone Serverless indexes can have latency spikes on infrequent access; keep-warm strategies add cost.

A KV store is a distributed hash map. Every value is stored under an opaque key; the store hashes the key to a shard (partition) and routes reads/writes to the shard's primary node. Redis keeps all data in RAM, optionally persisted to disk via append-only file (AOF) or periodic RDB snapshots. Lookup is O(1) independent of dataset size. Redis also supports richer data structures — hashes, sorted sets, streams — that map naturally to agent use-cases: sorted sets for priority queues, streams for event logs, hashes for session field storage.

A document store indexes JSON/BSON objects. MongoDB, for example, stores each document in a collection and builds a B-tree index on any field path — including nested fields and array elements. DynamoDB items are similar but limited to one primary index (partition key + optional sort key) plus up to 20 global secondary indexes (GSI). The critical design decision is the

: in DynamoDB you model the table around the queries, not the other way around. A common pattern for agent message history is PK = SESSION#{session_id} and SK = MSG#{ISO-timestamp}, which supports chronological scans and per-session range deletes efficiently.

Cassandra organizes data into partitions identified by the partition key. Within a partition, rows are sorted by clustering columns. This layout means that all rows with the same partition key live physically adjacent on disk, making range reads within a partition extremely fast. Writes are always appended to an in-memory structure (memtable) and a commit log before being flushed to immutable SSTables — making Cassandra write-optimized. Time-series event streams (agent tool-call audit logs) map naturally: partition by agent_id, cluster by event_time.

A vector database stores each document as a high-dimensional embedding — a float array produced by an embedding model. Retrieval is an approximate nearest-neighbor (ANN) search: given a query vector, return the k documents whose embedding vectors have the smallest cosine or Euclidean distance. Brute-force comparison is O(n * d) where d is the embedding dimension (often 1536). HNSW builds a multi-layer graph where each node is connected to its nearest neighbors; the search traverses from coarse to fine layers, achieving O(log n) query time at the cost of O(n * M) memory (M = connections per node, typically 16–64).

The diagram below shows a polyglot persistence architecture typical of a production agentic AI system. The agent orchestrator writes to each specialized store based on data type and access pattern. Postgres remains the authoritative store for billing and user data. An embedding pipeline asynchronously converts documents from object storage into vectors in the vector database.

attrs.alt: Polyglot persistence architecture for an agentic AI system showing Redis, Document Store, Vector DB, Wide-column, SQL, and S3

content.text: You need sub-millisecond session reads during every agent inference call.
You are rate-limiting tool calls per API key (atomic INCR + EXPIRE).
You need a lightweight job queue for async tool execution (Redis Streams or BullMQ).
You need pub/sub for streaming partial results to a frontend.
Conversation messages are nested JSON with evolving schemas (new tool types added over time).
You need per-session queries (all messages for session X) with infrequent cross-session analytics.
You are prototyping quickly and need to avoid schema migrations.
RAG retrieval requires semantic similarity search over more than ~50k chunks — pgvector sequential scan latency becomes unacceptable.
You need metadata filtering on top of ANN (filter by tenant_id AND find top-k similar).
Dedicated indexes (HNSW, ScaNN) offer lower latency SLAs than pgvector's IVFFlat.
Data has strong relational structure: users, subscriptions, invoices, runs with foreign keys.
ACID transactions are required — for example, billing a user and crediting a quota atomically.
Complex ad-hoc reporting queries cannot be pre-modeled as access patterns.

A practical heuristic: start with Postgres + pgvector + Redis. Migrate to a dedicated vector database when vector query latency exceeds 100 ms under load, and add a document database only when JSON schema evolution frequency or document size makes relational storage demonstrably painful.
Setting up Redis for session cache
Install the redis-py client: pip install redis
Connect to Redis (locally or via a managed service URL like Upstash).
Store session state as a Redis hash; set TTL to prevent unbounded growth.
Use SETNX (set-if-not-exists) for idempotency keys to prevent duplicate tool execution.

import redis
import json
from datetime import timedelta

r = redis.Redis(host="localhost", port=6379, decode_responses=True)

# Store a session as a Redis hash (TTL = 2 hours)
def save_session(session_id: str, data: dict, ttl_seconds: int = 7200):
    key = f"session:{session_id}"
    r.hset(key, mapping={"data": json.dumps(data)})
    r.expire(key, ttl_seconds)

def load_session(session_id: str) -> dict | None:
    key = f"session:{session_id}"
    raw = r.hget(key, "data")
    return json.loads(raw) if raw else None

# Atomic rate limit: allow at most 10 tool calls per minute per key
def check_rate_limit(api_key: str, limit: int = 10, window_seconds: int = 60) -> bool:
    rate_key = f"rate:{api_key}:tools"
    count = r.incr(rate_key)
    if count == 1:
        r.expire(rate_key, window_seconds)
    return count <= limit

# Idempotency key: prevent duplicate tool side effects
def claim_idempotency_key(idempotency_key: str, ttl_seconds: int = 86400) -> bool:
    """Returns True if this is the first claim; False if already executed."""
    result = r.set(f"idem:{idempotency_key}", "1", nx=True, ex=ttl_seconds)
    return result is True

The single-table design below stores conversation messages in DynamoDB. The partition key is SESSION#{session_id} and the sort key is MSG#{ISO-timestamp}, enabling efficient chronological retrieval of all messages in a session and TTL-based expiry.

import boto3
from datetime import datetime, timezone, timedelta
from decimal import Decimal
import json

dynamodb = boto3.resource("dynamodb", region_name="us-east-1")
table = dynamodb.Table("agent-conversations")

def put_message(session_id: str, role: str, content: list[dict]):
    now = datetime.now(timezone.utc)
    ttl_epoch = int((now + timedelta(days=30)).timestamp())
    table.put_item(Item={
        "PK": f"SESSION#{session_id}",
        "SK": f"MSG#{now.isoformat()}",
        "role": role,
        "content": json.dumps(content),   # tool calls, text blocks, etc.
        "created_at": now.isoformat(),
        "ttl": ttl_epoch,                 # DynamoDB removes item after 30 days
    })

def get_messages(session_id: str, limit: int = 50) -> list[dict]:
    """Return up to `limit` most recent messages for a session."""
    resp = table.query(
        KeyConditionExpression="PK = :pk AND begins_with(SK, :prefix)",
        ExpressionAttributeValues={
            ":pk": f"SESSION#{session_id}",
            ":prefix": "MSG#",
        },
        ScanIndexForward=False,   # newest first
        Limit=limit,
    )
    items = resp["Items"]
    items.reverse()               # chronological for the LLM
    return [{"role": i["role"], "content": json.loads(i["content"])} for i in items]
Example 1: vector similarity search with pgvector

pgvector extends Postgres with a vector column type and approximate nearest-neighbor operators. It is the lowest-friction starting point for RAG: the vector index lives in the same Postgres instance as the rest of the schema.

# pip install psycopg2-binary pgvector anthropic
import psycopg2
from pgvector.psycopg2 import register_vector
import anthropic
import json

conn = psycopg2.connect("postgresql://user:pass@localhost/agentdb")
register_vector(conn)

# Schema (run once):
# CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS vector;
# CREATE TABLE chunks (
#     id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
#     tenant_id TEXT NOT NULL,
#     text TEXT NOT NULL,
#     embedding vector(1536)
# );
# CREATE INDEX ON chunks USING hnsw (embedding vector_cosine_ops);

def embed(text: str) -> list[float]:
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()
    # Using a text-embedding-ada-002 via OpenAI or any embedding API
    # Placeholder: return a mock 1536-dim vector
    return [0.0] * 1536  # replace with real embedding call

def upsert_chunk(tenant_id: str, chunk_id: int, text: str):
    vec = embed(text)
    with conn.cursor() as cur:
        cur.execute(
            """INSERT INTO chunks (id, tenant_id, text, embedding)
               VALUES (%s, %s, %s, %s)
               ON CONFLICT (id) DO UPDATE
               SET text = EXCLUDED.text, embedding = EXCLUDED.embedding""",
            (chunk_id, tenant_id, text, vec),
        )
    conn.commit()

def retrieve(query: str, tenant_id: str, top_k: int = 5) -> list[str]:
    vec = embed(query)
    with conn.cursor() as cur:
        cur.execute(
            """SELECT text
               FROM chunks
               WHERE tenant_id = %s
               ORDER BY embedding <=> %s
               LIMIT %s""",
            (tenant_id, vec, top_k),
        )
        return [row[0] for row in cur.fetchall()]

# Usage
chunks = retrieve("how do I reset my password?", tenant_id="acme-corp", top_k=3)
print(chunks)
Example 2: MongoDB conversation history with full-document retrieval

MongoDB stores the entire conversation as a single document, making it trivial to fetch and update without joins. This pattern works well for conversations that always load in full and rarely need cross-conversation queries.

# pip install pymongo
from pymongo import MongoClient
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from typing import Any

client = MongoClient("mongodb://localhost:27017")
db = client["agentdb"]
sessions = db["sessions"]

# Ensure TTL index: MongoDB removes documents 30 days after expires_at
sessions.create_index("expires_at", expireAfterSeconds=0)

def create_session(session_id: str, user_id: str) -> None:
    sessions.insert_one({
        "_id": session_id,
        "user_id": user_id,
        "messages": [],
        "created_at": datetime.now(timezone.utc),
        "expires_at": datetime.now(timezone.utc).replace(
            day=datetime.now(timezone.utc).day + 30
        ),
    })

def append_message(session_id: str, role: str, content: Any) -> None:
    sessions.update_one(
        {"_id": session_id},
        {"$push": {"messages": {
            "role": role,
            "content": content,
            "ts": datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(),
        }}},
    )

def load_messages(session_id: str) -> list[dict]:
    doc = sessions.find_one({"_id": session_id}, {"messages": 1})
    if not doc:
        return []
    return [{"role": m["role"], "content": m["content"]} for m in doc["messages"]]

# Usage
create_session("s-001", user_id="u-99")
append_message("s-001", "user", [{"type": "text", "text": "What's the weather?"}])
append_message("s-001", "assistant", [{"type": "text", "text": "I'll check that now."}])
print(load_messages("s-001"))

A multi-tenant SaaS agent platform serving hundreds of enterprise customers illustrates the polyglot approach in practice. The platform runs an agent loop that handles customer support queries, executes retrieval, and calls external APIs on behalf of customers.

holds active session state for every open agent conversation. Each session key expires after 4 hours of inactivity. The platform uses a sorted set per tenant to implement a priority queue for inbound requests, and a Redis Stream for streaming partial responses to the browser via server-sent events.

stores the full message history partitioned by tenant_id and session_id. Items carry a TTL attribute set 90 days out. A Global Secondary Index on (user_id, created_at) lets the dashboard show a user's recent sessions without scanning the entire table. Conditional writes enforce that only one agent process can update a session at a time, preventing race conditions.

indexes 1536-dimension embeddings for each tenant's knowledge base. Namespaces in Pinecone provide tenant isolation: the retrieval query always targets namespace=tenant_id, preventing cross-tenant data leakage. The embedding pipeline runs on AWS Lambda triggered by S3 uploads, chunking documents into 512-token overlapping windows before embedding with a text-embedding model.

owns billing, subscription, usage quotas, audit summaries, and user accounts. A nightly job aggregates DynamoDB item counts into Postgres run records, which feed the billing engine. All financial writes use explicit transactions with SERIALIZABLE isolation to prevent double-billing.

The result: per-request session load in under 2 ms (Redis), message history queries in under 15 ms (DynamoDB, single partition), RAG retrieval in under 30 ms (Pinecone with HNSW), and billing reports that are fully consistent (Postgres ACID). No single database would satisfy all four requirements at this latency.
Treating a vector database as the source of truth

Embeddings are derived from source documents. If the source document changes or is deleted, the vector index goes stale and returns incorrect chunks at retrieval time. The source of truth must be object storage (S3) or a document store; the vector index is a derived index that can be rebuilt.

# WRONG: delete only the vector; the source document is still around
# and a re-index job will re-add this vector next run.
vector_index.delete(ids=["chunk-42"])

# RIGHT: delete source document first, then delete from vector index.
s3.delete_object(Bucket="docs", Key="tenant-1/file.pdf")
vector_index.delete(ids=["chunk-42"], namespace="tenant-1")

Document stores and KV caches cannot replace a relational database for financial data. Running billing aggregations across a MongoDB collection with no transactions means a crashed process mid-write can leave usage counts inconsistent. Keep money in Postgres.
Ignoring eventual consistency for idempotency keys

In an eventually consistent store, writing an idempotency key and immediately reading it on another node can return a cache miss, causing the same tool to execute twice. Use strongly consistent reads where supported (DynamoDB's ConsistentRead=True) or use Redis (which is strongly consistent for single-node deployments) for idempotency key storage.

Storing every message in a growing MongoDB document or DynamoDB item will eventually hit the 400 KB DynamoDB item size limit or balloon MongoDB document storage. Archive older messages to S3 and keep only the last N turns in the hot store, or use a separate item per message with a sort key.
Designing DynamoDB access patterns too late

DynamoDB's partition and sort key design is immutable after table creation. Teams that model the table around the domain first and access patterns second often discover they need a full table rebuild or expensive GSI additions. List all query patterns before writing the first item.
Assign each data category to the right store
— session state → Redis, message history → document/DynamoDB, embeddings → vector DB, billing → SQL. Document the mapping in an ADR.
— Redis EXPIRE on session keys, DynamoDB TTL attribute on messages, Pinecone vectors deleted when source is removed. Unbounded growth is the silent killer in agent stacks.
Enforce tenant isolation at the data layer
— DynamoDB: include tenant_id in the partition key. Pinecone: use namespaces. MongoDB: use separate collections or database-per-tenant. Never rely solely on application-level filtering.
Use strongly consistent reads for idempotency
— DynamoDB ConsistentRead=True adds latency but prevents duplicate tool side effects under retries.
Treat vector indexes as rebuild-able caches
— maintain a pipeline that can re-embed all source documents from scratch. Re-index after embedding model upgrades.
Monitor Redis memory with eviction policy awareness
— set maxmemory-policy to allkeys-lru on a session cache, but not on a job queue where eviction would silently drop work items.
— if all traffic hits one partition key (e.g., all writes go to SESSION#admin), throughput is capped. Distribute load with random suffixes or composite keys.
Prefer managed services for spiky workloads
— DynamoDB on-demand, Upstash Redis, and Pinecone Serverless scale to zero between spikes and remove cluster management.

A single Redis node handles 100k–1M simple GET/SET operations per second on commodity hardware. Redis Cluster shards data across nodes, scaling linearly with node count. For agent workloads, the bottleneck is usually network round-trip time (RTT), not Redis throughput — co-locate the agent process and Redis in the same VPC/availability zone.

DynamoDB provisioned throughput is per partition. Each partition handles up to 3,000 read capacity units and 1,000 write capacity units. With adaptive capacity, DynamoDB auto-redistributes within limits. At >10k writes/second on a single partition key, use partition key sharding: append a random 0–N suffix and scatter-gather on reads.

HNSW index query latency scales as O(log n) but memory scales as O(n * M * d) — a 1M-vector index at 1536 dimensions with M=16 consumes roughly 24 GB of RAM. Quantization (scalar or product) reduces memory by 4–8x with a small recall penalty. For agent RAG workloads, aim for recall@10 > 0.95 with p99 latency < 50 ms.

Cassandra scales write throughput horizontally by adding nodes; a 3-node cluster handles ~30k writes/second. Reads are fast within a partition; cross-partition reads require coordinator fan-out and should be avoided. Agent event streams map naturally: one partition per agent session or per hour, clustering by event_time.
— official Redis client; supports pipelines, Lua scripts, and Cluster mode.
— AWS SDK; DynamoDB resource API with automatic retries and pagination.
— Pinecone SDK for upsert, query, and namespace management.
— Weaviate Python client with schema-based collection management.
— Qdrant Python SDK with payload filtering and collection snapshots.
— VectorstoreRetriever wraps Pinecone, Weaviate, Chroma, pgvector, Qdrant with a uniform interface.
— VectorStoreIndex supports the same stores; adds document chunking and metadata filtering.
— async MongoDB driver for FastAPI/asyncio-based agent loops.

# Redis via Docker
docker run -d -p 6379:6379 redis:7-alpine

# MongoDB via Docker
docker run -d -p 27017:27017 mongo:7

# Qdrant locally (persistent volume)
docker run -d -p 6333:6333 -v $(pwd)/qdrant_data:/qdrant/storage qdrant/qdrant

# pgvector in Postgres
docker run -d -p 5432:5432 -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD=secret ankane/pgvector
Enable TLS for all connections (redis requirepass + TLS, MongoDB authSource, DynamoDB over HTTPS by default).
Rotate credentials via AWS Secrets Manager or Vault; never hardcode connection strings in agent code.
Pinecone and Weaviate Cloud authenticate via API keys; scope keys to read-only for retrieval-only services.

Multi-tenant agent systems must prevent cross-tenant data leakage at the database layer, not just the application layer. In DynamoDB, include tenant_id in the partition key and use IAM condition keys to restrict individual Lambda functions to their tenant's key prefix. In Pinecone, use separate namespaces or separate indexes per tenant for the strictest isolation.
DynamoDB: enable Point-in-Time Recovery (PITR); exports to S3 are available without consuming read capacity.
MongoDB Atlas: continuous backups with point-in-time restore up to 24 hours.
Redis: AOF persistence + periodic RDB snapshots; for critical data, replicate to a replica with AOF fsync=always.
Vector indexes: maintain the source embedding pipeline; rebuild the index from source documents rather than backing up vector binary blobs.
— in Sentinel or Cluster mode, promotion to replica takes 10–30 seconds; design agent sessions to tolerate a brief cache miss and reload from the document store.
— causes throttling; catch ProvisionedThroughputExceededException, back off with jitter, and alert on sustained throttling to trigger key design review.
— embeddings from a deleted document still return in queries until the delete propagates; implement soft-delete metadata filters as a safety net.
— in eventual-consistency setups, read-your-own-writes violations cause freshness bugs; use session consistency or read-after-write guarantees where available.
NoSQL is a family of four specialized engines — key-value, document, wide-column, and vector — each optimized for a distinct access pattern; it is not a replacement for SQL.

Production agent systems are polyglot: Redis for sub-millisecond session cache, a document store or DynamoDB for message history, a vector database for RAG retrieval, and Postgres for authoritative billing and user data.

Vector databases use approximate nearest-neighbor indexes (HNSW) to reduce similarity search latency from O(n) brute force to O(log n); start with pgvector and migrate to a dedicated store when query latency under load exceeds ~100 ms.
In DynamoDB, model the access patterns before writing the first item — the partition and sort key design is immutable and shapes all query capability.
Always set TTL on transient data (session keys, message history, idempotency keys) to prevent unbounded storage growth in Redis, DynamoDB, and vector indexes.
Use strongly consistent reads for idempotency keys (DynamoDB ConsistentRead=True, Redis single-node) to prevent duplicate tool side effects under retries.
Treat vector indexes as rebuild-able derived caches; the source documents in object storage are the authoritative data; re-embed after any model upgrade.
Enforce tenant isolation at the data layer (DynamoDB partition key prefix, Pinecone namespace, MongoDB database-per-tenant) rather than relying on application-level filtering alone.

Start simple: Postgres + pgvector + Redis covers most early-stage agent persistence needs with one ops team and consistent mental model; add specialized stores only when a measured bottleneck justifies the operational overhead.
